risk_of_bias.support_text: "Cluster randomization of the 34 primary care clinics was chosen to minimize contamination. Re-randomization balanced clinic attributes across 3 study groups on 2 primary factors: clinic urbanicity based on Rural-Urban Commuting Area (RUCA) codes, and the percentage of women at each clinic up to date on breast cancer screening to address clinic attentiveness to routine cancer screening." Referenced: Morgan KL, Rubin DB. Rerandomization to improve covariate balance in experiments. Ann Stat. 2012;40(2): 1263–82.
"The first concealed randomization scheme meeting balance criteria was selected and resulted in n = 11 CDS, n = 11 CDS+SDM, and n = 12 UC clinics."

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias): All outcomes
Blinding of outcome assessment (detection bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Not reported but outcomes were objectively measured and not subject to interpretation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Incomplete outcome data (attrition bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Flow diagram, low attrition rate and similar across arms (less than 10%), reasons for attrition recorded
The study protocol is available (NCT02986230) and all the study’s pre-specified primary and secondary outcomes that are of interest in the review have been reported in the pre-specified way.
Free of other potential biases: adjustment for clustering performed/no evidence of selective recruitment of cluster participants.

outcome: Finding Related To Ability To Use Decision Making Strategies

characteristics.method_raw:
Summary
Cluster‐randomized to decision aid + clinical decision support (CDS) vs CSD alone vs usual care

study_design: Cluster-randomized to decision aid + clinical decision support (CDS) vs CSD alone vs usual care
intervention_or_comparison: Cluster-randomized to decision aid + clinical decision support (CDS) vs CSD alone vs usual care

characteristics.participants: 34 clinics randomized in the USA: 11 DA + CDS (7807 patients), 11 CDS alone (8818 patients), 12 usual care (10,974 patients). Eligible patients were 1) aged 21 to 74 years; 2) not pregnant, cognitively impaired, or in hospice care; and 3) not up‐to‐date for breast, cervical, colorectal, or lung cancer screening at an index visit at a randomized clinic.
characteristics.interventions:
DA: paper‐based decision aids used in consultation plus web‐based clinical decision support (CDS). The decision aids included clinical information, probabilities of outcomes, explicit values clarification, and guidance in decision‐making. Short form versions of the decision aids notified patients and providers of the patients’ eligibility for a cancer screening test, briefly presented benefits and risks, options to consider, and invited the patient to access during the clinic visit the full‐length shared decision‐making tool and discuss with their provider. The CDS intervention was a web‐based, electronic health record‐linked system that included cancer prevention algorithms and the CDS output provided personalized recommendations to both primary care providers and patients in high‐literacy (provider) and low‐literacy (lay person) printed and electronic formats. The DAs are available as a supplementary appendix in the article.
Comparator: usual care (no details provided)
Comparator: CDS alone (not extracted)
characteristics.outcomes:
Primary outcome: a composite indicator of the proportion of patients overdue for breast, cervical, or colorectal cancer screening at index who were up to date on these 1 year later.
Secondary outcomes: breast cancer screening, cervical cancer screening, colorectal cancer screening, lung cancer screening.
characteristics.notes:
Source of funding: Financial support for this study was provided entirely by a grant from National Cancer Institute of the National Institutes of Health. The funding agreement ensured the authors’ independence in designing the study, interpreting the data, writing, and publishing the report. Research reported in this publication was supported by the National Cancer Institute of the National Institutes of Health under award No. R01CA193396.
Conflicts of interest: The authors declared no potential conflicts of interest with respect to the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

sections.text:
More than half of all cancer deaths could potentially be prevented or delayed by applying evidence-based primary and secondary cancer prevention interventions broadly to the U.S. population.[1–4] Despite many decades of research there is a wide and persistent gap between recommended preventive cancer care and care actually delivered in primary care settings, especially in rural areas.[3–8] [9–14] Thus, innovative approaches are urgently needed to improve primary and secondary cancer prevention care, especially in rural areas.

Cancer prevention often receives insufficient attention in primary care settings due to time constraints, competing priorities, ineffective CDS, patient and PCP skepticism or knowledge deficits related to cancer prevention, and evolving clinical guidelines based on many demographic, genetic, and behavioral factors.[15–17] In this scenario, CDS algorithms that process electronic health record (EHR) data to provide patient-specific care recommendations may facilitate translation of evidence-based guidelines into practice in a timely way and improve care.[18–24] Many EHR systems deliver simple prompts and reminders[25] that are insufficiently customized to individual patients and have limited impact on care.[21, 26, 27] More sophisticated and effective web-based EHR-linked CDS systems have been developed for various chronic conditions, but few such CDS systems focus on cancer prevention in rural settings.[18, 23]

The objective of this study was to improve evidence-based cancer prevention care in predominantly rural clinics by designing, implementing, and rigorously evaluating a web-based, EHR-linked CDS system based on United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) guidelines for primary and secondary cancer prevention.[28–35] Cancer prevention CDS was integrated within an existing CDS system that identifies and addresses other actionable clinical priorities at primary care visits, such as poorly controlled cardiovascular risk factors, and is described in detail elsewhere.[36, 37] In previous randomized controlled trials this CDS system had high use rates, high levels of PCP satisfaction, and improved various chronic disease care outcomes in specific groups of adult and pediatric patients.[18–24]

Current research suggests that shared decision-making (SDM) using decision aids may engage patients in productive discussion of treatment options with PCPs, but the impact of decision aids used in SDM on cancer preventive care is incompletely understood.[26, 38–42] Essentially, SDM involves a discussion between the patient and their provider that presents risks and benefits of alternative treatments/tests or none in a balanced manner, elucidates the patient’s preferences and goals, and engages the patient in decision making. Patient decision aids are tools to facilitate patients and providers in SDM. In this study SDM includes use of 2 types of Shared Decision Making Tools (SDMTs): a short-form SDMT designed to get the attention of patients and engage with their provider that was printed and delivered to all patient-provider dyads and a long-form SDMT available for use by interested dyads. Therefore, we designed the study to assess whether use of specific SDMTs [43, 44] may modify the effect of CDS on delivery of cancer prevention care, focusing on cancer screening tests.[45–49]

## Hypotheses, Study Design, and Study Setting

This 3-arm, parallel group, cluster-randomized trial was designed to test the hypothesis that providing point-of-care, web-based and EHR-linked CDS with or without SDMTs will improve specified cancer prevention outcomes as defined below.

As previously described,[37] this study was conducted from 08/01/2018 to 03/15/2020 at Essentia Health, a large integrated healthcare system that has used the EpicCare© (Verona, WI) EHR since 2004 and at the time of the study included 14 hospitals and 71 clinics in predominantly rural areas of northern Minnesota, eastern North Dakota, and northwestern Wisconsin. The largest thirty-four primary care clinics at Essentia Health participated in this study. Some existing EpicCare© reminders (Best Practice Alerts) for breast and colorectal cancer screening were available at all randomized clinics before and during the study but have been only marginally effective. [50]

## Randomization Procedure

Cluster randomization of the 34 primary care clinics was chosen to minimize contamination. Re-randomization balanced clinic attributes across three study groups on two primary factors:[51] clinic urbanicity based on Rural-Urban Commuting Area (RUCA) codes, and percentage of women at each clinic up to date on breast cancer screening to address clinic attentiveness to routine cancer screening. The first concealed randomization scheme meeting balance criteria was selected and resulted in n=11 CDS, n=11 CDS+SDM, and n=12 UC clinics.

## Patient and Primary Care Provider (PCP) Study Participants

At participating clinics around 300 PCPs provided care to approximately 400,000 patients; 17% of patients had Medicaid insurance and 74% of randomized clinics were rural based on RUCA2-UR codes. Eligible PCPs included physicians in general internal medicine or family medicine, and advanced practice providers engaged in primary care of non-pregnant adults.

Eligible patients were (a) aged 21 to 74 years, (b) not pregnant, cognitively impaired, or in hospice care, and (c) not up to date for breast, cervical, colorectal or lung cancer screening at an index visit at a randomized clinic. The index date was defined as the first visit during the accrual period at which a patient met eligibility criteria. Patients were assigned to the study group of the clinic of their index visit.[37]

## Data Sources and Variable Definitions

The primary outcome was a patient-level binary indicator of being up to date at 12 months following the index date on all cancer screenings (breast, cervical or CRC) that were overdue on the index date. The primary outcome was derived from manually triggered programmatic EHR data extractions, which captured all care recorded in the EHR from August 1, 2018, through March 15, 2020. Lung cancer was added to the protocol when the study began and is separately analyzed because at the time grant proposal was submitted, lung cancer screening recommendations had yet to be endorsed at the study site; therefore, lung cancer screening was not included as a primary outcome.

## Description of the CDS and CDS+SDM Interventions

The CDS intervention was a web-based, EHR-linked CDS system integrated within the primary care clinic workflow. Web-based CDS cancer prevention algorithms were based on USPSTF guidelines and CDS output provided personalized recommendations to both PCPs and patients in high-literacy (provider) and low-literacy (lay person) printed and electronic formats. [32–35] All recommendations were presented as suggestions, and the interface emphasized CDS suggestions do not take the place of clinical judgment or override a PCP’s detailed knowledge of a patient.[22]

In the CDS+SDM group, the same CDS materials were provided, along with short-form SDMTs for breast, colorectal, and lung cancer screening for all patients overdue for these screenings. At each CDS+SDM encounter when one of these cancer screenings was due, patients and PCPs received the indicated printed short-form SDMT(s). Short-form SDMTs (1/4-page for each condition) were shorter than long-form and given along with the previously described CDS output to all patients and PCPs in the CDS+SDM group. These short versions notified patients and providers of patients’ eligibility for a cancer screening test, briefly presented benefits and risks, options to consider, and invited the patient to access during the clinic visit the full-length SDMT and discuss with their provider. [Short-form SDMTs presented in Supplemental file X] Short-from SDMTs were implemented early in the study, because providers felt overwhelmed by the 3 to 4 page long-forms and requested a briefer short-form to introduce their patients to SDMTs if the visit allowed these discussions. Although all patient-provider dyads received the short-forms, only a subset of the dyads received the long-forms. All reported results included dyads that were potentially exposed to both short- and long-form SDMTs.

The full-length SDMTs for breast, colorectal, and lung cancer advised the patient and PCP to conduct cancer screening as recommended by the USPSTF. These SDMTs were developed using relevant IPDAS checklist criteria for cancer screening tests for primary care SDM.[52] These SDMTs were aligned with 35/37 IPDAS checklist criteria in the breast cancer and colorectal cancer screening tools and 31/37 criteria in the lung cancer screening tool. (For details see Supplemental section, file 8).[53] The long-form SDMTs are 3 to 4 page documents presented at the patient-provider clinic encounter, both in print and on the CDS interface. All eligible patients in the CDS + SDM group clinics received the short-form SDMT. However, only those patients and/or providers who chose to access the long-form SDMT received the printed form during the clinic visit with their provider.

The SDMTs were developed and refined iteratively with input from multiple stakeholders. This process started with the study investigative team using USPSTF recommendations to produce the initial tools that included primary care physicians, oncologist, psychologist, health economist, biostatistician, and health educators. These SDMTs were critically reviewed by performance site (EH) physicians, EH practice committee and leadership. Following literacy review and adaptation, the SDMTs were edited by HealthPartners’ patient education department, and pilot tested at two EH primary care clinics. After final revision, they were placed in production in the study. These SDMTs were created in 2016–17, pilot-tested in 2017–18, and used in the study 2018–2020. Because no changes occurred in the evidence supporting these SDMTs, no alterations were necessary during the study. These SDMTs were written at an 8th-grade literacy level.

The colorectal cancer screening long-form SDMT had four options: two stool-based tests, colonoscopy, and no screening test. The breast cancer screening long-form SDMT had two options: mammography or not but presented other decisions: age of onset and frequency of testing. The lung cancer screening long-form SDMT had two options: be tested or not. All SDMTs had insurance coverage information, value statements or question grids, and decision making with stepwise questions for the patient to complete. In addition, the SDMTs suggested that PCPs and patients use recommended cancer risk calculators to guide care. These risk calculators were easily accessible to the PCP through a link within the EHR but were not printed out with the CDS or SDMT materials. The long-form SDMTs included these features: explicit description of the decision; the rationale for screening test; presentation of options, benefits, and risk; values clarification; numerical probabilities; tailoring of information; and guidance in deliberation and communication. The development, implementation, and evaluation of the long-form SDMTs adhered to the SUNDAE checklist.[54] In the UC group, clinics and PCPs had no access to the CDS output or SDMTs.[50] The study design did not allow for documentation of whether the long-form SDMTs were given to or used by the patients. The SDMTs used in this study are included as a Supplemental file 9, and screen shots of CDS interfaces are presented in our published protocol paper and in Supplemental file X elsewhere. [18–24, 37]

## Training, Workflow, and Fidelity

Prior to the beginning of this cancer prevention study PCPs at study clinics had already been using EMR-integrated CDS tools to improve cardiovascular (CV) risk factor control in high-CV risk adults. The cancer prevention CDS protocol changed the CDS output to include cancer prevention recommendations, and prioritized cancer screening as a high priority for most study-eligible patients. The training provided to intervention clinic staff and clinicians briefly reinforced their use of the CDS tool, which was already in the 60–70% range prior to the beginning of the CPW-CDS update. Training emphasized how to access and use approved SDM tools for breast cancer, colon cancer, and lung cancer screening. We trained intervention clinics to use the CDS and SDMTs through strategies routinely used at Essentia Health, including one hour on-site and web-based training as previously described,[37] and additional clinic or PCP training as needed throughout the study when CDS use rates were low.

In both intervention group clinics (CDS and CDS+SDM), CDS training focused on workflow and identification of study-eligible patients using best practice alerts (BPAs) based on data automatically transmitted from the EHR to the web-service CDS algorithms. For study-eligible patient in intervention arms only, (a) a BPA indicating eligibility displayed to the rooming staff immediately after entering vital sign data in the EHR; (b) with two clicks, rooming staff viewed and printed the CDS, including SDMTs in CDS+SDM. (c) Rooming staff were instructed to hand the printed materials to the patient, saying “The list shows things that may help prevent cancer or heart disease. If you are ready to work on any of these things on this list, please talk with your doctor during your visit today.” (d) At intervention clinics a printed version of CDS (or CDS+SDM) materials was placed in the exam room basket or given to the PCP; these could also be displayed on the EHR screen with one click on the EHR navigator bar.[37] CDS and CDS+SDM output was suppressed if patients had an encounter with printed CDS within the prior two weeks. Strategies to promote ongoing use and fidelity to the intervention included: 1) on-site training on CDS and SDMT use just prior to the go-live date, 2) monthly email reports showing CDS use rates for each PCP and clinic, 2) ongoing communication by the study team with intervention clinic nurse managers, 3) support from Essentia Health leaders and clinic PCP leaders, 4) additional on-site lunch and learn sessions as needed for low-performing clinics, and 6) an incentive lunch for clinics with continued high CDS print rates. No monetary incentives were provided for use of CDS or SDMT.

The research team solicited ongoing, iterative feedback from stakeholders and measured both processes and outcomes as recommended by the CFIR and RE-AIM frameworks. [45, 46] and previously reported. [37, 47–49] Modifications were made because printing of full-length SDMTs was considered burdensome, which led to the implementation of an abbreviated ¼-page SDMT. We dealt with occasional printer failures at some clinics and observed variation in CDS and CDS+SDM workflows across clinics and in some cases across PCPs within a single clinic.

## Sample Size and Power

Onset of the COVID-19 pandemic led to marked decrease in routine outpatient care and near elimination of cancer screening tests at EH commencing 03/15/2020. In response to this event, the accrual period was truncated to end on 03/15/2019 so that 12-month follow-up would be complete as of 03/15/2020 before COVID-19 related disruptions to primary care. These changes reduced the original study accrual period from 15 months (08/01/2018 –10/31/2019) to 7.5 months (08/01/2018 – 03/15/2019).

A priori power calculations assumed data from 42,654 patients with visits to study clinics over 18 months who were eligible for cancer screening. Using original assumptions for the power calculations but with our actual sample size of 28,978 patients accrued over the shortened 7.5-month accrual period, this study was powered at 80% (2-sided test alpha level of .025) to detect a difference in the proportion of patients up to date for the composite screening endpoint of 18% in either active intervention group compared to 11% in UC. Power calculations assumed 10 clinics per study group with equal patient counts (n=966/clinic), ICC=0.02 for the composite endpoint, a pooled weighted average for men and women in the UC group of 11% up to date on cancer screening, and a generalized linear mixed model for analysis.

## Analytic Approach

Generalized linear mixed models with a logit link and binomial error distribution were used to test the effect of the intervention on the patient-level primary binary endpoint of up-to-date status on the composite of cancer screening by 12 months post-index date for breast, cervical, and colorectal cancer. The model included two study group terms representing contrasts for CDS vs. UC and CDS+SDM vs UC as originally assigned during randomization, pre-specified covariates of patient sex, age, insurance type, and number of cancer screenings due (ranging from 1–3), and covariates used in randomization: clinic-level urban/rural status, and percentage of patients prior to the study period with breast cancer screening up to date, and a random intercept for clinic. Tests of the two planned contrasts were 2-sided with P-values less than 0.025 considered statistically significant, and odds ratios for key study group contrasts report 97.5% confidence intervals. All other estimates report 95% confidence intervals. Comparisons of the CDS and CDS+SDM groups are exploratory and were not powered during study design. Models for the individual components of the composite endpoint (breast, cervical, colorectal cancer screening) and lung cancer screening follow the same form but remove some patient-level covariates. Analyses were conducted with SAS v9.4 (SAS Institute, Cary NC, 2016).

A sensitivity analysis included patient race (White vs other) as a covariate in the composite and lung cancer screening endpoint models. Pre-planned, unpowered heterogeneity of treatment effects analysis tested for differential effects of the intervention by patient sex for the composite, lung, and colorectal cancer screening endpoints by including interaction terms of patient sex and the study group contrast terms in the model.

## Protection of Human Subjects

The study was reviewed in advance, approved, and monitored by the Essentia Health Institutional Review Board (EH-16–1550). A waiver of written informed consent for study subjects was granted. Data Use Agreements and Business Associate Agreements governed data sharing between Essentia Health and HealthPartners Institute. The funder had no role in the study.

In total, 69,405 patients aged 21 to 74 had visits at the study clinics over the 7.5-month accrual period and were assessed for eligibility; 28,978 (41.8%) were due for one or more breast, cervical, or colorectal cancer screening (Figure 1). After exclusions documented in Figure 1, the analytic sample for the analysis of the composite cancer screening endpoint consisted of 25,198 patients, whose baseline characteristics are shown in Table 1. Among the 25,198 patients due for one or more screenings, 2053 (8.2%) were due for breast cancer screening only, 8420 (33.4%) cervical only, 10,247 (40.7%) colorectal only, 656 (2.6%) breast and cervical, 1858 (7.4%) breast and colorectal, 590 (2.3%) cervical and colorectal, 1374 (5.4%) breast, cervical, and colorectal cancer screening. The mean (SD) follow-up time from the index visit to the one-year follow-up data pull was 382 days (SD=8).

Among 25,198 patients with an index visit, the mean number of subsequent clinic visits was 1.9 (SD 2.16), and 28.2%, 71.8%, and 45.5% of those with an index visit had none, one or more, or two or more subsequent clinic visits respectively. Among the patients with a deficit in cancer preventive care at index visit, the CDS listed cancer preventive care as the top clinical priority in 86.6% of visits; the remainder of patients had cancer prevention listed as priority 2 to 6 of 6 behind one or more cardiovascular care recommendations.

The count of unique visits to the 34 study clinics by study-eligible patients over the 18-month period prior to randomization ranged from 581 to 13,379, with a mean (SD) of 5850 (3499). The percentage of patients with Medicaid coverage across the 34 clinics ranged from 5.9% to 27.4% with a mean (SD) percentage of 17.6% (6.0%). Pre-randomization data also showed the percentage of women aged 52–74 years with a mammogram in the past two years ranged from 59.8% to 93.8% across clinics with a mean (SD) of 77.2% (6.8%). There were no unanticipated negative consequences of the CDS or SDMTs reported.

## CDS Use and Print Rates

CDS interfaces were opened and printed at 50% of eligible clinical encounters in the CDS group, and at 66% of eligible clinical encounters in CDS+SDM group clinics. All patients in the CDS+SDM group received an appropriate short-form SDMT. However, we were unable to systematically observe or record the actual viewing and use of these printouts by PCPs and patients. In addition, we were unable to determine the proportion of patient-provider dyads that received and used the long-form SDMTs. In CDS+SDM group the link to the breast, colorectal, or lung cancer risk assessment tools was activated by PCPs at less than 1% of clinical encounters, despite robust print rates for the CDS and SDMT materials that recommended use of the risk calculators.

## CDS Use and Print Rates

### Composite Primary Endpoint of Breast, Cervical, and Colorectal Cancer Screening

Among 25,198 patients not up to date on breast, cervical, or colorectal cancer screening at the index date, 9,543 (37.9%) were up to date on the composite endpoint of all screenings that were due on the index date by one year following the index date. The adjusted, model-derived percentage of up-to-date status on the composite endpoint was 36.5% (95% CI: 34.0–39.1) for UC, 38.1% (95% CI: 35.5–40.9) for CDS, and 34.4% (95% CI: 31.8–37.2) for CDS+SDM (Table 2). Planned contrasts indicated that the CDS vs. UC groups (OR=1.07, 97.5% CI: 0.89–1.29, p=0.38) and CDS+SDM vs UC groups (OR=0.91, 97.5% CI: 0.76–1.10, p=.26) were not statistically significantly different on the composite endpoint. The adjusted odds ratio for the exploratory contrast comparing the CDS vs CDS+SDM study groups was 1.17 (95% CI: 1.00–1.38, p=0.056, Supplemental File 1). Odds ratios for all variables in adjusted models are presented (Supplemental File 2).

A sensitivity analysis including race as a covariate in the analysis for the composite screening endpoint yields odds ratios for study group contrasts virtually unchanged (CDS vs UC: OR = 1.08, CDS+SDM vs UC: OR = 0.92). However non-White patients were less likely to be up to date on the composite endpoint than White patients (OR = 0.72, 95% CI: 0.62 – 0.84, p<.001). Heterogeneity of treatment effects analysis indicated that the effect of study group on the composite screening endpoint did not vary by patient sex for the CDS vs UC comparison (interaction p=0.109) or CDS+SDM vs UC comparison (interaction p=0.94).

## CDS Use and Print Rates

### Individual Breast, Cervical, and Colorectal Cancer Screening

There were N=5,941 study-eligible patients due for breast cancer screening, 11,040 due for cervical cancer screening, and 14,069 (n=7,029 women and n=7,040 men) due for colorectal cancer screening (Table 2; CONSORT diagrams in Supplemental Files 3, 4, 5). Screening was completed by one year following the index date for 2770 (46.6%) patients for breast cancer, 4,368 (39.6%) for cervical cancer, and 5,088 (36.2%) for colorectal cancer. For patients in CDS vs. UC clinics, the adjusted odds ratios for the individual cancer screenings making up the composite were similar to the composite endpoint, with OR=1.03 (97.5% CI: 0.78–1.37) for breast cancer screening up to date, 1.06 (97.5% CI: 0.78–1.43) for cervical cancer screening up to date, and 1.11 (97.5% CI: 0.85–1.45) for colorectal cancer screening up to date. Similarly, for patients in CDS+SDM vs. UC clinics, the adjusted odds ratios for the individual cancer screenings making up the composite were similar in magnitude to the composite endpoint, with OR=0.91 (97.5% CI: 0.69–1.21) for breast cancer screening up to date, 0.91 (97.5% CI: 0.68–1.23) for cervical cancer screening up to date, and 0.89 (97.5% CI: 0.68–1.16) for colorectal cancer screening up to date.

The effect of study group on colorectal cancer screening did not vary by patient sex for the CDS vs UC comparison (interaction p=0.69) or CDS+SDM vs UC comparison (interaction p=0.39).

## CDS Use and Print Rates

### Lung Cancer Screening

A total of 35,373 patients aged 55–74 years had visits at the 34 study clinics over the 7.5-month period of patient accrual and were assessed for lung cancer screening by the CDS algorithms (Supplemental File 6), and 2149 (6.1%) were due for lung cancer screening. Patients not due for lung cancer screening (n=33,224) were ineligible for analysis, as were 69 patients removed due to hospice care or cognitive impairment at index date.

Among the 1864 analysis-eligible patients not up to date on lung cancer screening at the index date 502 (26.9%) were up to date on lung cancer screening by one year following the index date. The adjusted, model-derived percentage of up to date status on lung cancer screening for UC was 22.1% (95% CI: 16.4–29.0), CDS was 30.6% (95% CI: 23.3–39.0), and CDS+SDM was 22.4% (95% CI: 16.1–30.3) (Table 2). The adjusted odds ratio comparing CDS vs UC study groups was 1.55 (97.5% CI: 0.84–2.86, p=0.099) and for CDS+SDM vs UC was 1.02 (97.5% CI: 0.54–1.93, p=.94). The adjusted odds ratio for the exploratory contrast comparing the CDS vs CDS+SDM study groups was 1.53 (95% CI: 0.88–2.65, p=0.130, Supplemental File 1). The effect of study group on lung cancer screening did not vary by patient sex for the CDS vs UC comparison (interaction p=0.36) or CDS+SDM vs UC comparison (interaction p=0.47).

Including race as a covariate yields odds ratios for study group contrasts unchanged (CDS vs UC: OR = 1.55, CDS+SDM vs UC: OR = 1.02), and shows that non-White patients and White patients do not differ significantly on the likelihood of being up to date on lung cancer screening (OR = 0.81 [reference=White], 95% CI: 0.39 – 1.68, p=0.57).

## CDS Use and Print Rates

### Exploratory Analyses

We unexpectedly observed directionally lower screening rates in the CDS+SDMT group versus both the UC group and the CDS group for the composite and most individual screening endpoints. Supplemental File 1 reports that the CDS group had 17%, 13%, 16%, 24%, and 52% higher screening rates than the CDS+SDMT group for the composite primary outcome, breast cancer, cervical cancer, colorectal cancer, and lung cancer screening, respectively, although these differences did not reach statistical significance in this study with its Covid-truncated accrual period.

The results reported here suggest that the cancer prevention care recommendations provided through this CDS system with and without SDMTs did not significantly improve breast, cervical, colorectal, or lung cancer prevention care. A clinically significant positive association, albeit statistically non-significant, was seen in the comparison of lung cancer screening up-to-date status of 31% in the CDS group and 22% in the UC group (OR=1.55) in a study that had a truncated enrollment period and therefore reduced power due to the Covid-19 pandemic.

CDS systems similar to the one we evaluated have been shown to be effective in improving practice behaviors and patient outcomes in various other clinical domains, such as hypertension, cardiovascular risk reduction, and diabetes management in primary care. [18–24] Therefore, future work should focus on ascertaining whether CDS for cancer prevention is best designed differently or similarly to CDS designed to address chronic disease management. We integrated cancer prevention CDS with CDS for multiple other clinical domains, in the belief that such an approach is more patient-centered and more likely to be used compared to deploying multiple disease-specific CDS tools at a relatively brief primary care encounter.[55] Resolving this issue is important and will require additional qualitative research. Results from our patient interviews are reported separately,[56] and indicate that patients generally viewed the CDS output favorably. Efforts to provide CDS output directly to patients prior to or after clinical encounters via EHR patient portals or other pathways may merit exploration.

SDMTs have received much attention over the last decade.[38–42, 57] Many studies of SDMT use in primary care settings have reported improved knowledge and decisional processes, but effects on clinical outcomes have been mixed.[42, 58, 59] Two studies on SDMTs for colorectal cancer screening showed no increase in screening, while one study reported a small effect.[60–62] One study on SDMTs for lung cancer showed no effect.[63] Thus, our overall results are consistent with other studies evaluating SDM for cancer screening test completion in primary care.[64, 65] Studies of CDS in rural settings are very sparse, with none reported on cancer screening tests. The only prior CDS study conducted in a rural setting in the U.S. targeted atrial fibrillation management and reported no effect on clinical outcomes.[66]

In this study, we observed directionally lower screening rates in the CDS+SDMT group compared to the CDS group for the primary composite outcome and for all four cancers, although these findings did not reach statistical significance. To the degree that SDMTs clarify patients’ values and reduce decisional conflict, it is possible that objective data on benefit and risks of certain screening procedures may reduce some patients’ willingness to proceed with certain evidence-based screening procedures. [42, 67]

Another issue related to SDMTs was the number of pages or displays required to present these tools to patients and PCPs. Up to eight printed pages of long-form SDMTs could be presented to patient-provider dyads in the CDS+SDM group assuming a patient was eligible for three screening tests and related SDMTs. Many rooming staff and some PCPs in CDS+SDM clinics found that amount of SDMT information too extensive to review with patients at brief clinical encounters typically scheduled for purposes other than cancer prevention. Apparently due to this issue, printing of intervention materials in CDS+SDM clinics dropped an absolute 20% initially but increased substantially in CDS+SDM after short-form SDMTs were implemented, and ultimately surpassed print rates in the CDS clinics. These print rate data suggest that SDMT simplicity and brevity are valued in primary care settings. The impact of simpler versus more complex SDMTs, and the impact of various types of SDM training on SDMT use and patients’ decision making is an ongoing area of active research. [38–41] As reported in other studies, PCPs do not have enough time to address preventive care services,[68] and as reported by Solberg et al, improving prevention care in busy primary care settings is difficult. [53, 54, 64–66, 69]

## Limitations

Several factors limit interpretation of these data. The study was conducted at one relatively high-performing predominantly rural healthcare system, and generalizability of results to other care systems or patient populations is uncertain. The intervention was delivered during clinic encounters with PCPs; the impact of the CDS delivered in other clinical venues or directly to patients using mobile health technologies may be greater or less than what we observed. CDS and SDMT training was limited by available time, as described above, and more intensive training strategies may be considered in the future. [40] We estimated CDS use based on print rates, but a weakness of this approach is assuming PCPs and patients received these printouts and used them. It was beyond our study scope to conduct direct observation of CDS use at the 22 intervention clinics. Although 66% of the patients in the CDS+SDM group were exposed to the short-form SDMTs, a smaller and unknown proportion of these patient-provider dyads were exposed to the long-form SDMTs. Therefore, our study may not adequately evaluate the impact of these SDMTs. We responded to COVID-19 disruptions to clinic-based care in this study by truncating the accrual period from 15 months to 7.5 months, decreasing statistical power. Although the development of the SDMTs aligned substantially with the IPDAS checklist criteria, they may not be optimal tools in their current iteration. Participating providers in the study reported that these SDMTs were too long and not easy to use with their patients. Although the study development and evaluation complied with nearly all SUNDAE checklist items, the study lacked process methods and evaluation to better understand how and why SDMTs worked or did not.[54]

Our study also had some strengths that merit acknowledgement. The long-form SDMTs were based on relevant principles articulated by the IPDAS collaboration with adherence to most of the criteria,[52, 53] and recommendations in the CDS and SDMTs were consistent with each other and with then-current USPSTF cancer prevention recommendations. The development and evaluation of the SDMTs adhered to the majority of SUNDAE checklist items.[70] The CDS design adhered to the principles of effective CDS systems,[71] including the “10 Commandments” promulgated by Bates et al,[72] and a successful implementation strategy described by Sperl-Hillen et al.[22–24] [73–76] This CDS intervention simultaneously standardized and personalized cancer screening recommendations,[77] was designed to engage both patients and PCPs; and was designed to fit with clinic workflow and rely on rooming staff rather than PCPs to trigger the CDS, print CDS interfaces and SDMTs, and deliver them to PCPs and patients immediately before the exam room encounter. Implementation included multiple training points and methods; and subsequently monthly monitoring and feedback of CDS print rates were sent to PCPs and their clinic managers. This scalable CDS approach uses web-based algorithms that are easy to maintain as evidence evolves and facilitates CDS and SDMT dissemination to other healthcare systems.[73–76]

Additional work is needed to optimize the impact of CDS with or without SDMTs on cancer prevention care in primary care settings. Future efforts should focus on optimizing PCP and patient use of CDS tools and modifying and perhaps simplifying SDMT design. Studies are needed to assess whether additional patient access to CDS (with or without SDMT) before or after visits by using EHR patient portals or mobile communication may further improve cancer prevention care.

We would like to acknowledge Drs. Benjamin Crabtree and Kurt Stange for serving as external consultants to this study.

Funding

Research reported in this publication was supported by the National Cancer Institute of the National Institutes of Health under Award Number R01CA193396. The content is solely the responsibility of the authors and does not necessarily represent the official views of the National Institutes of Health.

Financial support for this study was provided entirely by a grant from National Cancer Institute of the National Institutes of Health. The funding agreement ensured the authors’ independence in designing the study, interpreting the data, writing, and publishing the report.

Funding source: NIH, NCI grant. R01 CA193396
